section_title: INTRODUCTION TO MASTERING HIGH PROBABILITY SCALPING VOL. 1
content:
Welcome to volume one of three for Mastering High Probability Scalping. This video will be kept on my
YouTube channel. I ask the trading community to help me by reporting instances where my content is re
- uploaded on other YouTube channels. I put the work into these presentations and would like to get the
credit and benefit of ad revenue. If you see it, please let me know via email at innercircletrader@gmail.com.
I greatly appreciate it.

section_title: DEFINING HIGH PROBABILITY SCALPS AND ICT'S TRADING STYLE
content:
What is high probability scalps? For me as ICT, it's ten to thirty pip price swings. I don't do a lot of this type
of trading; it's only recently adopted for showcasing talents and generating study material. It's an easy way
to get people excited, but it's not the only way to trade. What I teach has consistency and proves markets
are not random, but very organized and specific in their intraday and weekly targets. My precision doesn't
extend to longer terms. I like day trading and short-term trading. 'One shot one kill' is what I'm known for,
trading the weekly range. Daily highs and lows, and weekly highs and lows are my specialty. I trade inside
those ranges, aiming for the 'meat' of the move, not the very high or very low. As long as extremes haven't
been met, there's opportunity.

section_title: VOLUME 1 FOCUS AREAS AND STUDY APPROACH
content:
This presentation will be concise, as it's part of three volumes. Always have a notepad, turn off distractions,
and listen. I'm packing 20+ years of information and experience into a small bandwidth.
- Write down your questions; answers will likely be in future presentations.
In this volume, we'll be highlighting:
- Many opportunities in scalping the Forex.
- How to learn directional bias for higher timeframe institutional sponsorship (explained later).
- How to determine the highest probable times of the day to trade.
- How to frame high probability setups for runs on liquidity.

section_title: UNDERSTANDING HIGH PROBABILITY SCALPING WITH AN EXAMPLE
content:
This is an example of a high probability scalp in the Dollar Cad. This was a weekly high at the time of
presentation. Everything shown in this specific range is necessary for short-term intraday scalping.
Because it's scalping, setups may sometimes overlap with longer-term conditions. I'm not teaching 'one
shot one kill' swing trading or position trading here, but I will drop nuggets for you to study, found in my free
tutorials on innercircletrader.com. You can join the free forum, watch tutorials, and see my trading journal
entries to understand my interpretations and reflections on market actions.

A short term scalp is a very simple approach to running out previous day's highs or previous day's lows.
That's all it is. We're targeting buy stops above the previous day's high or a day or two ago. We're always
going to be looking back the last three days. This is your range. You'll be looking back at the highest high
and the lowest low in the last three days. You're counting today as day one. For instance, if today is
Thursday, we would be looking at Wednesday's data and Tuesday's data.

section_title: ADOPTING AN INSTITUTIONAL MINDSET FOR CONSISTENCY
content:
We're aiming for exactly how bank traders trade. I'm going to show you something no one else teaches. My
presentations have become more concise and accurate because I've spent 14 months sharing openly what
I've learned over 24+ years. It's not retail. I'm going to show you how to leave retail stuff behind and think
institutionally. I want everyone who follows me to leave that retail mindset because it will be a hindrance
and prevent consistency. If you follow retail methods, you're likely not consistent. Using the things I teach in
these three volumes will quickly help you find consistency.

I did not say profitability. I cannot promise that. Some of you are going to break the rules, do things early or
late, risk too much, and do too many things at one time. That's what's going to cause the adverse effect. I
don't want the credit for your wins, and I don't want the credit for your losses, so be responsible. This
content is for informational purposes only.

When we look at high probability scalping, this is an hourly chart on the Dollar Cad. I've highlighted the
individual daily highs and lows. From a scalping standpoint, your primary timeframe will be the daily, as it
gives you a bias. For looking for liquidity, you'll use the hourly chart, which provides a nice framework to
see previous day's highs or highs from two days ago. For instance, the far right shows Thursday's data.
Yesterday was Wednesday's data, with its low and high highlighted. Then Tuesday's high and low. We've
traded below Wednesday's low today and also below Tuesday's low. This is a significant point I'll discuss
more in Volume 2, but I'm bringing it to your attention now as it's salient for tomorrow's trading.

section_title: BULLISH PRICE ACTION AND LIQUIDITY RUNS
content:
Each day's daily highs and lows. When the market is bullish (defined purely by price action, no indicators),
we believe higher prices are in order. An institutional mindset involves running liquidity on the previous
day's high or previous days prior to yesterday's high. The reason is that there are many speculations about
'catching highs' in the marketplace. Especially if we had a day with a retracement lower the day before or
two days ago, there will be built-in short positions. Folks who sold short will want to capitalize on movement
lower. Where will their buy stops be? Above the most recent high. What's the most recent high? Yesterday's
or the day before.

section_title: THE ROLE OF BUY STOPS AND SMART MONEY
content:
Why look back two days ago and include today's range? Because every swing high and swing low on a
daily chart is created by three individual bars. You must monitor these daily highs and lows to eventually
forecast swing highs and lows before they materialize, as I'll teach in Volume 3. Sometimes markets turn
quickly without the setup outlined in Volume 1. This is the bread and butter, easy way approach. It won't
give you a setup every single trading day, which is good. Avoid trying to trade every day. You can use a
demo account daily to practice, but don't force your live account to trade every day. Day trading is not 'every
day' trading.
- When your stop gets hit, it becomes a market order to buy. Your buy stop transforms into a market order
to buy at the market. The market is driven up there from an algorithmic standpoint (or supply and demand if
that helps you). When price goes above an old high, it's forcing buyers to come in at a higher price, instead
of buying low. Smart money has accumulated positions at lower prices and is driving price up to create
opportunities for participants with existing orders or real interest to buy at a higher level, forcing them into
or out of positions. This whole discussion is framed on looking for buy-side liquidity or buy stops, or running
out buy-side liquidity pools.

section_title: FOCUSING ON ONE CURRENCY PAIR FOR MASTERY
content:
I started as a Treasury bond trader and S&P futures trader. Those were my two markets of choice. I was
rarely in both because they are diametrically opposed, like the Dollar Index and a foreign currency. I didn't
trade anything else. I focused on those two markets because I didn't need anything else. If you are
watching multiple pairs right now, stop. Humor me for one month. I promise you, if you give me one month
of your time and focus on one currency pair (it doesn't have to be one I suggest, pick any major crossed
with the dollar), you will learn more by doing that than trying to do all these other things with different pairs.
If you're trading multiple assets and just starting to learn forex, stop trading other asset classes and focus
one month with me. Use the information I give you, and I guarantee you will understand price far better
than you ever have before, with very simple approaches.

section_title: THE DRAW ON LIQUIDITY IN BULLISH MARKETS
content:
So, if we're bullish, what we want to be thinking about is where price is going to be drawn to. That's called
the 'draw'. When we look for where the market is going to reach for in terms of bullishness, it's the previous
day's high or an old high that's going to be in the form of buy-side liquidity or buy stops. So, the market will
draw up to that level and dip into that liquidity pool where there's existing buy stops. For scalping, we're
using the previous day's high for a very simple little day trading approach to capture 10 to 30 pips inside of
one trading day.
